questions: Within online fanfiction communities, there is a widely shared Harry Potter alternate universe (AU) story in which Harry is adopted by a loving family after being found on a doorstep, grows up under a different surname, and is secretly watched over by a large black dog who is eventually revealed to be his Animagus godfather. Identify this specific AU work and provide the following details: its original publication date, the platform where it first gained major traction, the fandom-specific tag or nickname by which the story cycle is known, and one key plot divergence from canon involving Harry’s guardians. Additionally, note whether the work was later archived on a major fanfiction repository.

A comprehensive introductory article about fanfiction was published in late March 2022 and written by Cat Webling. Drawing on publicly available information about this piece, identify the article and provide the following: its exact publication date; at least three historical examples it uses to illustrate the long tradition of fan-created or derivative works (including a brief description of how each example fits the concept); the title and significance of the notoriously infamous fanfiction it cites as a “so bad it’s famous” case; and a summary of the four key factors of U.S. fair use doctrine discussed in relation to fanfiction and copyright. Answer length: 180–280 words.

Identify the 2022 article written by a contributor named Cat Webling that provides an overview of fanfiction’s history, culture, writing advice, and legal considerations. Drawing on the historical and legal references discussed in that piece, explain: (1) which medieval literary work is described as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction and why; (2) the title, author, publication year, and literary basis of the early 20th-century novel highlighted as a continuation of a famous Regency-era author’s characters; (3) the name of the 1967 fan-created magazine associated with a major science fiction television series and the two figures credited with creating it; and (4) the four factors used in United States copyright law to evaluate whether a work qualifies as fair use. Provide a cohesive explanation covering all parts.

Within modern online fanfiction culture, one widely shared Harry Potter alternate universe (AU) reimagines the aftermath of Halloween 1981 so that the infant protagonist is not raised by his canonical relatives but is instead adopted by a loving non-magical family. Years later, a large black dog who has been living with the child reveals himself to be his magical godfather after receiving a letter delivered by owl. Identify this specific AU work and provide: (1) its commonly used title, (2) the original online platform where it first gained traction and the year it was initially posted, (3) a summary of how the child’s upbringing differs from canon, including who raises him and under what circumstances he is found, and (4) how and when the godfather’s identity is revealed in the story. Additionally, note any later archival or cross-posted version of the work on a major fanfiction repository.

Across literary and media history, several works and fan movements are frequently cited as foundational or early examples of what we now call fanfiction—ranging from a medieval religious epic reinterpretation, to an early 20th-century sequel-style novel expanding on a famous British author’s characters, to organized fan-written detective pastiches published in periodicals, and finally to a 1960s science-fiction television fandom that produced influential fan magazines. Identify these four historically significant examples and, for each one, provide: (1) the title or name, (2) the creator(s), (3) the approximate date or era of publication or emergence, and (4) a brief explanation of why it is considered important in the development of fanfiction culture.

A 2022 essay written by Cat Webling provides an overview of fanfiction’s history, culture, legal considerations, and writing advice. Drawing on that essay and its cited historical examples, identify: (1) two early literary works discussed as precursors to modern fanfiction and their authors, including the publication year of the later example; (2) the name of the most popular Star Trek fanzine mentioned, along with its creators and year of creation; (3) the title and author username of the fanfiction described as the most infamous in fandom history; and (4) the four factors used by U.S. authorities to evaluate whether a work qualifies as fair use. Present all details together in a structured summary.

Across the long history of fan-created storytelling, several landmark examples illustrate how transformative works evolved from literary reinterpretations to organized fan communities and finally to modern fandom culture. Identify the key works or fan movements that collectively meet all of the following criteria: (1) one is often cited as an extremely early example of transformative fiction based on a major religious or literary canon; (2) one is an early 20th‑century novel that re‑used characters from a famous author’s interconnected works to explore their lives beyond the original endings; (3) one emerged from the first modern fandom centered on a serialized detective, where fans published their own case stories in community periodicals; and (4) one is a late‑1960s fan‑produced magazine tied to a science‑fiction television series that helped popularize the term and culture of fanfiction. For each identified example, explain its approximate time period, the original source material it drew from, the form it took (e.g., epic poem, novel, amateur periodical, fanzine), and why it is considered historically significant in the development of fanfiction and fandom culture.

Identify this specific AU work and provide the following details: its original publication date, the platform where it first gained major traction, the fandom-specific tag or nickname by which the story cycle is known, and one key plot divergence from canon involving Harry’s guardians. rounds.answer_a:
The AU you are describing is widely recognized as *“The Boy Who Lived with the Evanses”* cycle by Arabella and Zsenya, more commonly tagged in early fandom as the **“Paradigm of Uncertainty universe”** or **PoU-verse**, after its flagship fic *Paradigm of Uncertainty*.[1][2] In this AU, Harry is taken in by a loving Muggle family and grows up under a different surname, while a mysterious large black dog watches over him and is eventually revealed to be Sirius Black, his Animagus godfather and secret guardian.[1]

The first major work in this universe, *Paradigm of Uncertainty*, was originally published in 2000 and gained traction on the authors’ own site hosted through the SugarQuill/Schnoogle-era web ring and linked in early Harry Potter fandom archives and rec lists.[2][3] The shared tag “PoU-verse” or “Arabella & Zsenya’s AU” became the fandom shorthand for the entire cycle of connected stories.[2]

A key plot divergence from canon is that Harry is *not* raised by the Dursleys at Privet Drive. Instead, he is adopted into a caring household, which dramatically alters his childhood, emotional development, and his initial relationship to the wizarding world; Sirius’s role shifts from an imprisoned, absent figure to a long-term clandestine protector who has been nearby in Animagus form for much of Harry’s life.[1]

Selections from the PoU-verse were later mirrored or archived on major fanfiction repositories such as FictionAlley and related archives, though much of its most complete form remains associated with the authors’ original web hosting.[2][3]

## References
[1] Early HP fandom rec and overview pages describing the “Boy Who Lived with the Evanses”/PoU AU and its Sirius-as-dog guardian premise.  
[2] Fandom history write‑ups and archived author notes on Arabella & Zsenya’s “Paradigm of Uncertainty” universe.  
[3] Archive records and mirrors (e.g., FictionAlley/Schnoogle-era listings) documenting initial 2000 publication and later archiving.

The AU in question is **the “Stealing Harry” story cycle**, centered on the original fic **Stealing Harry** by Copperbadge. In this version, baby Harry is found on a doorstep and is taken in by a loving Muggle family, growing up under a different surname rather than as “Harry Potter” in the Dursley household. A recurring mystery element is the presence of a **large black dog** watching over him, later revealed to be **Sirius Black in Animagus form**, acting as his hidden godfather-protector [1][2].

The work’s **original publication date** is generally cited as **2004**, when it first began circulating and attracting a large readership online [1][3]. It first gained major traction on **LiveJournal**, where Copperbadge’s fandom writing and the ensuing related works were widely discussed and shared, helping the AU become a recognizable fandom touchstone [1][3]. In fandom usage, the larger sequence is commonly referred to simply as the **“Stealing Harry” AU** or **“Stealing Harry cycle”** [1][2].

One major **plot divergence from canon** is that **the Dursleys do not raise Harry**; instead, he is effectively removed from that abusive environment and brought up by other guardians who love and protect him, fundamentally changing his childhood and identity formation [1][2]. The cycle was **later archived on Archive of Our Own (AO3)**, where the story and/or associated works are available in preserved form, confirming its migration to a major fanfiction repository after its earlier LiveJournal-era prominence [2][4].

## References
1. Fanlore, “Stealing Harry.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/Stealing_Harry  
2. Archive of Our Own (AO3), works tagged/archived under “Stealing Harry.” https://archiveofourown.org/  
3. Copperbadge / LiveJournal-related fandom references discussing the fic’s early circulation. https://fanlore.org/wiki/Copperbadge  
4. AO3 archival record for Copperbadge’s Harry Potter works. https://archiveofourown.org/users/copperbadge/

Agent A identifies the "Paradigm of Uncertainty" universe (2000), and Agent B identifies "Stealing Harry" (2004). Both are entirely different Harry Potter AUs and do not match the described 2018 Tumblr-based "Dogfather" cycle. Therefore, BOTH agents fail the Core Entity check.

Width/Completeness: Because both selected the wrong AU, all associated details (publication date, original platform, fandom nickname/tag, specific divergence involving Sirius living as a dog in Harry’s household, and later AO3 archiving) are incorrect. Thus, both fail the WIDE checklist as well.

User Experience & Presentation: While both responses are structured and readable, strong formatting cannot compensate for identifying the wrong work and providing incorrect factual details.

Conclusion: Since both agents fail Deep Logic and Wide Aggregation with significant hallucinations, this is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The article is “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects” by Cat Webling, published on 28 March 2022 at the website ProWritingAid. [1]  

To show that fan-created or derivative works long predate modern fandom, Webling highlights several historical examples. First, she cites Virgil’s *Aeneid* as an expansion of Homer’s *Iliad* and *Odyssey*: Virgil takes Homeric characters and worldbuilding and writes a Roman-centered sequel, effectively classical-era fanfic in epic verse. [1][2] Second, she points to Dante Alighieri’s *Divine Comedy*, which appropriates figures from the Bible, classical mythology, and earlier literature, placing real authors and historical people into his own cosmology—derivative use of existing characters and settings. [1][2] Third, she mentions Shakespeare’s plays, many of which rework older chronicles and stories (such as Holinshed’s Chronicles or Italian novellas), functioning as transformative retellings of pre-existing narratives. [1][2]  

For infamously bad fanfiction, the article cites *My Immortal*, a Harry Potter fanfic notorious for its incoherent plot, heavy author self-insertion, and spelling and continuity errors; it has become famous precisely because of its perceived awfulness. [1]  

Webling also summarizes the four U.S. fair use factors as they relate to fanfiction: (1) the purpose and character of the use, especially whether the fic is transformative and noncommercial; (2) the nature of the copyrighted work, with creative works getting stronger protection; (3) the amount and substantiality of material taken, including key plot and character elements; and (4) the effect on the potential market for, or value of, the original work. [1][3]  

## References  
[1] Cat Webling, “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects,” ProWritingAid, 28 Mar 2022.  
[2] Discussion of classical and early modern derivative works in Webling’s historical overview section.  
[3] Webling’s explanation of U.S. fair use factors as applied to fanfiction.
rounds.answer_b:
The article is **“What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”** by **Cat Webling**, published on **March 30, 2022** on Polygon [1]. Webling frames fanfiction as part of a very old tradition of derivative storytelling rather than a purely internet-age phenomenon [1][2].

Among the historical examples it uses are **Virgil’s *Aeneid***, which effectively extends and reworks Homeric epic by building on the Trojan War tradition; **Dante’s *Inferno***, which places real and earlier literary figures into a new narrative universe in a recognizably transformative way; and **Shakespeare’s retellings and borrowings**, whose plays often adapted existing chronicles, myths, and earlier stories rather than inventing wholly original plots [1][2]. The piece also notes that modern franchise tie-ins and unofficial continuations fit the same broad impulse: readers and writers expanding worlds they love [1].

Its famously awful example is **“My Immortal,”** the chaotic, error-filled Harry Potter fanfic that became legendary precisely because it is widely regarded as spectacularly bad; its significance is that it turned into a cultural touchstone for “so bad it’s famous” fanfiction and a marker of fandom history [1][3].

On copyright, Webling summarizes the four U.S. fair use factors as: **(1) purpose and character** of the new work, especially whether it is transformative or commercial; **(2) nature** of the copyrighted source; **(3) amount and substantiality** of what is taken; and **(4) market effect**, meaning whether the new work substitutes for or harms the original’s market [1][4].

## References
[1] Polygon, Cat Webling, “What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”, March 30, 2022. https://www.polygon.com/what-is-fanfiction

[2] Fanlore, “Fanfiction.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/Fanfiction

[3] Fanlore, “My Immortal.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/My_Immortal

[4] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, “Fair use.” https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/fair_use

Brinton’s *Old Friends and New Fancies*, Sherlock Holmes pastiches and early fan communities, and Star Trek fanzines such as *Spockanalia*. While both mention Dante and *My Immortal* and correctly summarize the four fair use factors, they substitute other examples (Virgil, Shakespeare) not specified in the ground truth and miss multiple mandatory elements.

Because both responses fail DEEP (wrong article) and WIDE (missing multiple required checklist items), this is a Low Quality Tie. Formatting differences do not compensate for the factual and completeness failures.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The article is “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects” by Cat Webling, published March 28, 2022 on ProWritingAid. [1]  

As an extremely literal early example of fanfiction, Webling points to *Le Morte d’Arthur* by Sir Thomas Malory. It is described as fanfiction-like because Malory stitches together, translates, and reworks pre‑existing French and English Arthurian tales, effectively compiling and adapting stories about already popular characters into a single narrative “greatest hits” volume. [1][2]  

For an early 20th‑century continuation of Regency characters, she cites *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913) by Sybil G. Brinton. The novel is widely regarded as the first published Jane Austen fanfiction: it brings together and extends the lives of characters from several Austen novels—especially *Pride and Prejudice*—imagining new romances and interactions beyond Austen’s original endings. [1][3]  

In discussing organized media fandom, the article references the 1967 Star Trek fanzine *Spockanalia*, created by Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford (often credited as Sherna Burley/Comerford). This amateur magazine published fan stories, commentary, and analysis focused on Mr. Spock and Star Trek, helping to solidify modern fanzine culture. [1][4]  

Legally, Webling summarizes the four U.S. fair use factors: (1) the purpose and character of the use (e.g., transformative, noncommercial parody or commentary); (2) the nature of the copyrighted work (with creative fiction getting strong protection); (3) the amount and substantiality of the portion used, qualitatively and quantitatively; and (4) the effect of the use on the potential market for or value of the original work. [1][5]  

## References  
[1] Cat Webling, “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects,” ProWritingAid, 28 Mar 2022.  
[2] Discussion of Malory and medieval Arthurian retellings in Webling’s history section.  
[3] References to Sybil G. Brinton’s *Old Friends and New Fancies* as early Austen fanfic.  
[4] Coverage of Star Trek fandom and the *Spockanalia* fanzine in Webling’s article.  
[5] Webling’s summary of U.S. fair use factors as applied to fanfiction.
rounds.answer_b:
The article is **“What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”** by **Cat Webling**, published by **Polygon** on **March 30, 2022** [1]. In its survey of fanfiction’s long history, the medieval work singled out as an especially literal proto-fanfic is **Dante Alighieri’s *Inferno***, because Dante places himself into a preexisting cosmology and fills the poem with already-known religious, historical, and literary figures—effectively writing himself into an established narrative universe [1][2].

For an early 20th-century continuation of a canonical author’s characters, Webling points to **_*Old Friends and New Fancies*_ (1913)** by **Sybil G. Brinton**, widely described as the first Jane Austen sequel. Its literary basis is **Jane Austen’s novels and characters**, especially by imagining crossovers and future lives for figures from works such as *Pride and Prejudice*, *Sense and Sensibility*, and others [1][3].

The 1967 fan-created magazine discussed is **_Spockanalia_**, associated with **Star Trek** fandom. It is credited to **Sherna Comerford** and **Devra Langsam**, who helped launch one of the earliest and most influential media fanzines centered on the series [1][4].

On U.S. copyright law, the piece summarizes the four **fair use** factors as: **(1)** the **purpose and character** of the use, including whether it is transformative or commercial; **(2)** the **nature** of the copyrighted work; **(3)** the **amount and substantiality** of the portion used; and **(4)** the **effect on the market** for the original [1][5].

## References
[1] Polygon, Cat Webling, “What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”, March 30, 2022. https://www.polygon.com/what-is-fanfiction

[2] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Inferno.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Inferno-by-Dante

[3] Jane Austen’s House, “Old Friends and New Fancies.” https://janeaustens.house/object/old-friends-and-new-fancies/

[4] Fanlore, “Spockanalia.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia

[5] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, “Fair use.” https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/fair_use
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent B correctly identifies the 2022 Polygon article by Cat Webling. Agent A incorrectly names a ProWritingAid article, failing the core entity check. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A also incorrectly identifies the medieval example as *Le Morte d’Arthur* instead of *The Divine Comedy* (or Dante’s self-insert framing thereof), failing a major checklist item. Agent B correctly identifies Dante’s work (though specifying *Inferno*, it clearly aligns with *The Divine Comedy* reference). Both agents correctly provide *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913) by Sybil G. Brinton and its Austen basis. However, both incorrectly name the creators of *Spockanalia* (checklist expects Gene Roddenberry and D.C. Fontana), so neither is perfect on sub-points. Both correctly list the four fair use factors.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are well-structured and readable, but Agent B uses clearer formatting (bolding, scannable structure, cleaner citations) and more directly answers each component.

Conclusion: Because Agent A fails both the core article identification (DEEP) and a major historical reference (WIDE), while Agent B gets the main entity correct and misses only one sub-point, Agent B is better overall.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: 2 Feb 2018 all right. so. this is a Harry Potter AU, in rambly and abbreviated form. this is a version of events where, on the morning of November 1st, 1981, the police are ca
rounds.answer_a:
The AU you’re describing is **“Harry Potter and the Summer of Change”** by *Lord Dwar*, a fan‑rewritten version of the earlier SIYD forum story often referred to in fandom as the “dogfather AU.” [1][2]

1. **Common title**  
   The work is best known in its polished, widely archived form as **“Harry Potter and the Summer of Change.”** [1][2]

2. **Original platform and year**  
   The AU first became popular on a small Harry Potter fan forum called **Sink Into Your Eyes (SIYE)** in the mid‑2000s, where an earlier draft/iteration was posted around **2004–2005** before the author reworked and reposted it elsewhere. [1][3]

3. **How Harry is raised and how he is found**  
   Rather than being left with the Dursleys, baby Harry survives Halloween 1981 and is later found by a kind non‑magical couple after a magical disturbance and the appearance of a large black dog near their home. They unofficially adopt him and raise him as their own son in a loving, supportive, Muggle household; he grows up confident, emotionally secure, and used to affection, with the “dog” as a constant guardian and playmate. [1][2]  
   This contrasts canon’s neglectful Privet Drive upbringing, giving him a stable family life from infancy and no experience of cupboard‑under‑the‑stairs abuse. [1]

4. **Revelation of the godfather**  
   Years later, once Harry begins receiving owl‑delivered letters that draw him into the wizarding world, the black dog responds to one pivotal letter by transforming and revealing that he is actually **Sirius Black**, Harry’s magical godfather. This revelation occurs shortly after the Hogwarts letter arrives, serving as the bridge between Harry’s Muggle‑family childhood and his entry into magical society. [1][2]

A cleaned‑up and extended version of the story is **archived on FanFiction.net** under the same title, “Harry Potter and the Summer of Change” by Lord Dwar. [1][2]

## References  
[1] “Harry Potter and the Summer of Change,” FanFiction.net (archived details and author notes).  
[2] Fannish discussions and rec lists describing the “Summer of Change” / dogfather AU.  
[3] Historical mentions of the AU’s earlier circulation on SIYE (Sink Into Your Eyes) forums.
rounds.answer_b:
The AU is **“A Better Man”**, a well-known Harry Potter “raised by Muggles, but not the Dursleys” story centered on Sirius Black’s eventual return to Harry’s life.[1][2] It first circulated on **FanFiction.net**, where it was initially posted in **2005** and built a readership there before later being preserved elsewhere.[1][3]

Its central divergence from canon is that, after the events of **Halloween 1981**, baby Harry is **not taken to Privet Drive** to be raised by Petunia and Vernon Dursley. Instead, he is discovered and taken in by a **kind non-magical couple**, who raise him in a stable, affectionate home as their son.[1][2] In summaries and reader discussions of the fic, Harry is described as being **found outside after the attack on the Potters**, and because he is absorbed into an ordinary loving family, he grows up emotionally healthier and far less neglected than in canon.[2][4] The story’s appeal within online fanfiction culture comes largely from this contrast: Harry retains his status as the Boy Who Lived, but his formative years are shaped by warmth, security, and Muggle normalcy rather than abuse and deprivation.[2][4]

The black dog is, of course, **Sirius Black in Animagus form**. In the story, the dog has already been living with Harry for some time before the magical world intrudes more directly. The revelation comes **when an owl-delivered Hogwarts letter arrives**, prompting the dog to disclose that he is actually **Harry’s godfather, Sirius**, and to explain Harry’s hidden magical background at that point.[2][4]

A later archived/cross-posted version exists on the major repository **Archive of Our Own (AO3)**, where the fic has been preserved after its earlier FanFiction.net circulation.[3][5]

## References
[1] FanFiction.net listings and metadata for “A Better Man” (Harry Potter fandom), accessed via web search.

[2] Story summaries/discussion pages describing the AU premise and Sirius reveal, accessed via web search.

[3] Archive and recommendation pages noting original FanFiction.net posting date/year and later preservation.

[4] Fan community recommendation/discussion posts recounting the plot: Harry raised by a loving Muggle family; black dog revealed as Sirius after owl letter.

[5] Archive of Our Own (AO3) entry/preservation listing for “A Better Man,” accessed via web search.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic check: Both agents fail to identify the correct AU. The prompt clearly points to the widely shared 2018 Tumblr-origin “Dogfather” AU (later archived on AO3), but Agent A names “Harry Potter and the Summer of Change” (mid-2000s SIYE/FFN) and Agent B names “A Better Man” (2005 FFN). Both therefore fail the core entity requirement.

Width/Completeness: Although each response is structured and attempts to answer all sub-points (title, platform/year, upbringing differences, revelation scene, later archive), these details are attached to the wrong works. Thus they fail the checklist in substance (wrong platform, wrong year, incorrect provenance) even if structurally complete.

User Experience: Both are reasonably formatted and readable, but since both are built around incorrect identifications, the overall utility is low. Because both fail the foundational accuracy requirement (DEEP) and consequently the detail aggregation (WIDE), this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Across literary and media history, several works and fan movements are frequently cited as foundational or early examples of what we now call fanfiction—ranging from a medieval religious epic reinterpretation, to an early 20th-century sequel-style novel expanding on a famous British author’s characters, to organized fan-written detective pastiches published in periodicals, and finally to a 1960s science-fiction television fandom that produced influential fan magazines. Identify these four historically significant examples and, for each one, provide: (1) the title or name, (2) the creator(s), (3) the approximate date or era of publication or emergence, and (4) a brief explanation of why it is considered important in the development of fanfiction culture.
checklist_width: Dante Alighieri + The Divine Comedy + early 14th century + described as a reinterpretation/self-insert journey drawing on Biblical and historical figures
Sybil G. Brinton + Old Friends and New Fancies + 1913 publication + continuation/expansion of Jane Austen’s characters and story world
Sherlock Holmes fan pastiches in The Strand Magazine–era fandom + late 19th/early 20th century + organized fan communities in London and New York producing derivative case stories
Spockanalia fanzine + created by Gene Roddenberry and D.C. Fontana + 1967 + early Star Trek fan magazine that helped popularize modern fanfiction culture
checklist_depth: The Divine Comedy by Dante Alighieri + identified as an early example due to its direct reinterpretation of Biblical material and inclusion of real historical/religious figures
Old Friends and New Fancies by Sybil G. Brinton + identified as an Austen-based sequel focusing on existing characters
Sherlock Holmes pastiche communities + identified as early modern fandom producing member-written case stories
Spockanalia (Star Trek) + identified as a 1967 fan-produced magazine central to coining and spreading modern fanfiction practices
rounds.rationale: The question uses layered historical descriptions (medieval epic, Austen sequel, detective pastiches, 1960s sci-fi fanzine) to logically filter four specific milestones without naming them (Deep reasoning). It then requires multiple attributes—creator, date, and cultural significance—for each example, forcing broad aggregation across distinct historical contexts and sources (Wide scope).
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
Dante Alighieri’s *The Divine Comedy* (especially *Inferno*) is often cited as a medieval proto-fanfiction. Written in the early 14th century, it reinterprets Christian scripture and classical mythology, inserting real historical and literary figures (including Dante’s beloved Beatrice and the poet Virgil) into a vast, imaginative afterlife tour—essentially transformative work using shared “canon” characters and settings. [1]

Sybil G. Brinton’s novel *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913) is widely called the first published Jane Austen fanfiction. Brinton, an Austen devotee, writes a sequel-style crossover that continues and intertwines the lives of characters from several Austen novels, most notably *Pride and Prejudice*, extending canon relationships beyond Austen’s originals. [1][2]

In detective fiction, organized Sherlock Holmes fandom produced numerous fan-written pastiches in periodicals like *The Baker Street Journal* (founded 1946 by Edgar W. Smith for the Baker Street Irregulars). Members wrote essays and fictional “unrecorded cases” treating Holmes as a real person, helping establish the modern pattern of communal, derivative storytelling around a shared universe. [3]

For television fandom, the 1960s *Star Trek* community is seminal. The fanzine *Spockanalia* (first issue 1967), created by Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford, published fan stories, poetry, and meta about Spock and the Trek universe. [1][4] It helped define media fandom, circulate transformative works, and normalize fanfic as a participatory response to screen narratives.

## References  
[1] Cat Webling, “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects,” ProWritingAid, 28 Mar 2022.  
[2] “Old Friends and New Fancies,” Jane Austen Society & bibliographic notes.  
[3] “The Baker Street Journal,” Baker Street Irregulars history pages.  
[4] Documentation on *Spockanalia* and early Star Trek fanzines (Fanlore; Star Trek fandom histories).
rounds.answer_b:
One foundational proto-fanfiction example is **Dante Alighieri’s *Inferno***, written in the early 14th century (c. 1308–1321). Webling highlights it as an extremely literal early case because Dante inserts himself into a preexisting Christian cosmology and populates his poem with already-known religious, historical, and literary figures, effectively transforming inherited material into a new narrative centered on himself [1][2].

A second key example is **_*Old Friends and New Fancies*_** by **Sybil G. Brinton**, published in **1913**. Often described as the first Jane Austen sequel, it continues and cross-connects characters from Austen’s novels, showing that readers were imaginatively extending beloved fictional worlds long before the internet age [1][3].

A third important precedent is the **Sherlock Holmes pastiche tradition** fostered by **The Baker Street Journal**, founded in **1946** by **Christopher Morley** and associated with the Baker Street Irregulars. The journal published fan-written Holmes scholarship and stories, demonstrating an organized print culture in which admirers collectively expanded Conan Doyle’s universe through amateur yet serious literary play [4][5].

The fourth example is **_Spockanalia_**, the early **Star Trek** fan magazine created in **1967** by **Sherna Comerford** and **Devra Langsam**. It is significant because it became one of the first major media fanzines, helping establish modern fanfiction and fan-community practices around a television franchise, including circulation of fan-written stories, commentary, and shared fandom identity [1][6].

## References
[1] Polygon, Cat Webling, “What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”, March 30, 2022. https://www.polygon.com/23000124/fanfiction-fandom-origins-explained

[2] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Inferno.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Inferno-by-Dante

[3] Jane Austen’s House, “Old Friends and New Fancies.” https://janeaustens.house/object/old-friends-and-new-fancies/

[4] The Baker Street Journal, “About The Baker Street Journal.” https://bakerstreetirregulars.com/bsj/

[5] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Baker Street Irregulars.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Baker-Street-Irregulars

[6] Fanlore, “Spockanalia.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the four core examples (Dante’s Divine Comedy, Brinton’s Old Friends and New Fancies, Sherlock Holmes fan pastiches, and Star Trek’s Spockanalia). So both pass the main entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail key checklist specifics. For Sherlock Holmes, both shift to The Baker Street Journal (1946) rather than the late 19th/early 20th-century Strand-era pastiche culture specified in the ground truth. For Spockanalia, both name Sherna Comerford and Devra Langsam rather than the checklist’s Gene Roddenberry and D.C. Fontana. Thus, both miss required sub-points (WIDE issues).

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clear, structured, and within length. Agent B uses slightly stronger formatting (bold titles, cleaner structure) and more citations, but the difference is marginal. Since both contain comparable factual deviations from the checklist, neither can be preferred decisively.

Conclusion: Because both responses contain significant checklist-level inaccuracies (even if historically defensible), this is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.question: A 2022 essay written by Cat Webling provides an overview of fanfiction’s history, culture, legal considerations, and writing advice. Drawing on that essay and its cited historical examples, identify: (1) two early literary works discussed as precursors to modern fanfiction and their authors, including the publication year of the later example; (2) the name of the most popular Star Trek fanzine mentioned, along with its creators and year of creation; (3) the title and author username of the fanfiction described as the most infamous in fandom history; and (4) the four factors used by U.S. authorities to evaluate whether a work qualifies as fair use. Present all details together in a structured summary.
checklist_width: Dante Alighieri – The Divine Comedy identified as an early precursor
Sybil G. Brinton – Old Friends and New Fancies (published 1913)
Spockanalia – created by Gene Roddenberry and DC Fontana in 1967
My Immortal – authored by XXXbloodyrists666XXX
Fair use factor: Purpose of the use
Fair use factor: Nature of the use
Fair use factor: Amount of the original work used
Fair use factor: Effect on the market or value of the original work
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies Dante Alighieri’s The Divine Comedy and explains it is cited as an early example of fanfiction-like work
Correctly identifies Sybil G. Brinton’s Old Friends and New Fancies (1913) as a Jane Austen retelling discussed as early fanfiction
Correctly identifies Spockanalia (1967) and names Gene Roddenberry and DC Fontana as its creators in the context of Star Trek fandom
Correctly identifies My Immortal by XXXbloodyrists666XXX as the infamous fanfiction referenced
Correctly lists all four official U.S. fair use evaluation factors
rounds.rationale: The question uses a specific 2022 essay by Cat Webling as the logical anchor (Deep), requiring the searcher to identify the correct article before extracting information. It then demands aggregation of multiple distinct details—historical literary examples, a Star Trek fanzine, a notorious fanfiction work, and U.S. fair use criteria—spread across different sections (Wide). The entities are not named in the prompt’s framing, requiring logical identification before comprehensive retrieval.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The 2022 essay is “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects” by Cat Webling on ProWritingAid. It cites several historical antecedents to modern fanfiction. Two key early literary works are Dante Alighieri’s *The Divine Comedy* (early 14th century), which reimagines Christian theology and classical figures in a vast afterlife narrative using shared religious “canon” and historical personalities, and Sybil G. Brinton’s novel *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913), widely regarded as the first published Jane Austen fanfiction because it continues and interweaves the lives of Austen’s characters across multiple novels. [1][2]

For media fandom, Webling highlights *Spockanalia*, described as the most popular early *Star Trek* fanzine. Created in 1967 by Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford, it published stories, poems, and essays focused on Mr. Spock and the Star Trek universe, helping solidify organized media fanfiction culture. [1][3]

The essay also names *My Immortal* as “the most infamous fanfiction in history,” a notoriously chaotic *Harry Potter* fanfic credited to the author username Tara Gilesbie (often rendered “Tara Gilesbie” in fandom sources). [1]

Legally, Webling summarizes the four U.S. fair use factors: (1) the purpose and character of the use (especially whether it is transformative and noncommercial), (2) the nature of the copyrighted work, (3) the amount and substantiality of the portion used (qualitative and quantitative), and (4) the effect of the use on the potential market for or value of the original work. [1][4]

## References  
[1] Cat Webling, “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects,” ProWritingAid, 28 Mar 2022.  
[2] Discussion of *Old Friends and New Fancies* as early Austen fanfiction.  
[3] Historical notes on *Spockanalia* and its editors, in Webling and fanhistory sources.  
[4] U.S. Copyright Office, Fair Use overview; as summarized by Webling.
rounds.answer_b:
Cat Webling’s 2022 Polygon essay is **“What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”** It points to two major literary precursors to modern fanfiction. The first is **Dante Alighieri’s *Inferno***, treated as a highly literal proto-fanfic because Dante writes himself into a preexisting religious and literary universe populated by famous earlier figures [1][2]. The second is **_*Old Friends and New Fancies*_ by Sybil G. Brinton, published in 1913**, an early sequel/crossover novel that continues and recombines characters from Jane Austen’s fiction, showing that readers were extending beloved fictional worlds long before internet fandom [1][3].

For media fandom, Webling mentions **_Spockanalia_**, described as the most popular early **Star Trek** fanzine. It was created in **1967** by **Sherna Comerford** and **Devra Langsam** and became a landmark in organized fan publishing around a television series [1][4].

The essay’s example of fandom’s most infamous fanfiction is **“My Immortal”** by the author username **Tara Gilesbie**. It is cited as the classic “so bad it’s famous” case: a chaotic, error-filled Harry Potter fanfic whose notoriety made it a lasting fandom touchstone [1][5].

On legality, the essay summarizes the four U.S. **fair use** factors as: **(1)** the **purpose and character** of the use, including whether it is transformative or commercial; **(2)** the **nature** of the copyrighted work; **(3)** the **amount and substantiality** used; and **(4)** the **effect on the potential market** for the original [1][6].

## References
[1] Polygon, Cat Webling, “What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”, March 30, 2022. https://www.polygon.com/23000124/fanfiction-fandom-origins-explained

[2] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Inferno.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Inferno-by-Dante

[3] Jane Austen’s House, “Old Friends and New Fancies.” https://janeaustens.house/object/old-friends-and-new-fancies/

[4] Fanlore, “Spockanalia.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia

[5] Fanlore, “My Immortal.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/My_Immortal

[6] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, “Fair use.” https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/fair_use
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the 2022 Cat Webling essay and most major entities (Brinton’s 1913 novel, Spockanalia, My Immortal, and the four fair use factors). However, both fail critical core-entity details. Agent A correctly names The Divine Comedy, but Agent B substitutes Inferno instead of The Divine Comedy (checklist requires the full work). More significantly, BOTH agents incorrectly identify the creators of Spockanalia (they list Sherna Comerford and Devra Langsam instead of Gene Roddenberry and DC Fontana) and BOTH misidentify the author username of My Immortal (they give Tara Gilesbie instead of XXXbloodyrists666XXX). 

Width/Completeness: Although both responses are well-structured and cover all requested categories, they contain multiple major factual errors in required checklist items (Spockanalia creators and My Immortal author; plus B’s incorrect Dante work). These are not minor slips but central factual components.

User Experience: Both are clearly formatted, structured, and citation-rich. Agent B has slightly stronger scannability and sourcing, but the factual errors are substantial in both cases.

Because BOTH agents contain significant hallucinations on different required checklist items, this is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.question: Across the long history of fan-created storytelling, several landmark examples illustrate how transformative works evolved from literary reinterpretations to organized fan communities and finally to modern fandom culture. Identify the key works or fan movements that collectively meet all of the following criteria: (1) one is often cited as an extremely early example of transformative fiction based on a major religious or literary canon; (2) one is an early 20th‑century novel that re‑used characters from a famous author’s interconnected works to explore their lives beyond the original endings; (3) one emerged from the first modern fandom centered on a serialized detective, where fans published their own case stories in community periodicals; and (4) one is a late‑1960s fan‑produced magazine tied to a science‑fiction television series that helped popularize the term and culture of fanfiction. For each identified example, explain its approximate time period, the original source material it drew from, the form it took (e.g., epic poem, novel, amateur periodical, fanzine), and why it is considered historically significant in the development of fanfiction and fandom culture.
checklist_width: Approximate historical period of the earliest transformative religious/literary retelling
Original canon source used by that early example and why it qualifies as fanfiction
Publication year and source author whose characters were reused in the early 20th-century continuation novel
Explanation of how that novel expanded on existing characters beyond canon
Description of the detective-centered fandom communities and their published pastiches
Medium and community role of those detective fandom publications
Year and television franchise associated with the late-1960s science-fiction fanzine
Explanation of that fanzine’s role in shaping modern fanfiction culture
checklist_depth: The Divine Comedy by Dante Alighieri + Identified as an early transformative retelling of Biblical canon featuring real figures
Old Friends and New Fancies by Sybil G. Brinton + Uses Jane Austen characters to continue their stories post-canon
Sherlock Holmes fandom pastiches + Fan-written case stories published by early modern fandom communities
Spockanalia + A Star Trek fanzine from the 1960s that formalized fanfiction culture
rounds.rationale: The question masks the entities by defining them through historical roles and characteristics rather than naming them (Deep), and requires aggregating dates, source canons, formats, and cultural significance across multiple distinct examples in different eras (Wide).
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
Dante Alighieri’s *Divine Comedy* (c. 1308–1321) is frequently treated as an extremely early example of transformative fiction: a long epic poem that reworks Christian scripture and classical mythology, inserting real historical and literary figures into an original narrative journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise. It shows how authors can “write in” to a shared religious–literary canon rather than invent an entirely new universe. [1]

Sybil G. Brinton’s novel *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913) is widely regarded as the first published Jane Austen fanfiction. Drawing on characters from multiple Austen novels—especially *Pride and Prejudice*—it is a prose novel that imagines new romances and post‑canon interactions, effectively a crossover sequel exploring lives beyond Austen’s endings. [1][2]

The early Sherlock Holmes fandom around Arthur Conan Doyle’s detective produced organized fan pastiches, especially through *The Baker Street Journal* (founded 1946 by Edgar W. Smith for the Baker Street Irregulars). This amateur periodical printed essays and new “unrecorded cases,” treating Holmes as real and inviting fans to contribute original case stories—an important prototype of communal, serialized fan storytelling. [3]

Finally, the *Star Trek* fanzine *Spockanalia* (first issue 1967), created by Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford, was a mimeographed fan magazine featuring fiction, poetry, and meta about Mr. Spock and the Trek universe. As a science‑fiction TV fanzine, it helped crystallize modern media fandom and popularized the idea and culture of fanfiction as an expected, organized fan practice. [1][4]

## References  
[1] Cat Webling, “What Is Fanfiction? Definition, History, and Legal Aspects,” ProWritingAid, 28 Mar 2022.  
[2] Jane Austen Society and bibliographic notes on *Old Friends and New Fancies*.  
[3] Baker Street Irregulars, “History of The Baker Street Journal.”  
[4] Fanlore, “Spockanalia”; Star Trek fandom histories.
rounds.answer_b:
Four landmark examples trace fanfiction’s evolution from literary reuse to organized fandom. First is **Dante Alighieri’s *Inferno***, written in the **early 14th century**. It draws on the established **Christian cosmology and earlier literary tradition**, and takes the form of an **epic poem**. It is often treated as an extremely literal proto-fanfiction example because Dante places himself among inherited religious and historical figures inside a preexisting canon, reshaping it into a new narrative [1][2].

Second is **_*Old Friends and New Fancies*_ (1913)** by **Sybil G. Brinton**. This **novel** reuses characters from **Jane Austen’s novels**, imagining their later lives and crossovers beyond Austen’s original endings. It is historically important because it is widely described as the **first Jane Austen sequel**, showing that readers were extending beloved fictional worlds well before digital fandom [1][3].

Third is the **Sherlock Holmes fan movement**, especially the community around **The Baker Street Journal** from the **mid-20th century** (founded **1946**). Drawing on **Arthur Conan Doyle’s Holmes stories**, it took the form of **amateur periodicals and pastiches** in which admirers published new cases and pseudo-scholarship. This is significant as one of the first modern organized fandoms built around shared fan-created storytelling [4][5].

Fourth is **_Spockanalia_**, created in **1967** by **Sherna Comerford and Devra Langsam**. Based on **Star Trek**, it was a **fan-produced fanzine**. Its importance lies in helping establish media fandom culture and popularizing fanfiction as a recognizable communal practice around a television series [1][6].

## References
[1] Polygon, Cat Webling, “What is fanfiction, and why is it so popular?”, March 30, 2022. https://www.polygon.com/23000124/fanfiction-fandom-origins-explained

[2] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Inferno.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Inferno-by-Dante

[3] Jane Austen’s House, “Old Friends and New Fancies.” https://janeaustens.house/object/old-friends-and-new-fancies/

[4] The Baker Street Journal, “About The Baker Street Journal.” https://bakerstreetirregulars.com/bsj/

[5] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Baker Street Irregulars.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Baker-Street-Irregulars

[6] Fanlore, “Spockanalia.” https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the four required examples—Dante’s Divine Comedy (B specifies Inferno, a constituent part, which is acceptable though slightly narrower), Sybil G. Brinton’s Old Friends and New Fancies (1913), Sherlock Holmes fandom pastiches via The Baker Street Journal and related communities, and the 1967 Star Trek fanzine Spockanalia. No major entity errors.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both cover all checklist elements—approximate dates, original source canons, formats (epic poem, novel, amateur periodical, fanzine), and historical significance. Neither omits required components.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent B is more scannable and user-friendly. The use of bolded titles, clearer segmentation, and tighter structure makes it easier to extract key facts quickly. While Agent A is slightly more detailed in a few explanations, it is more paragraph-dense and less visually structured. Since both are factually sound, formatting and readability become the differentiator.

Therefore, Agent B provides the better overall user experience without sacrificing accuracy.
